segments.s769-g41: شبان را: را is the definite/direct-object marker attached to شبان, so شبان is the thing/person being put on the throne. همی ... کرد: the combination همی (an archaic/progressive particle) with the past verb کرد gives an older, continuous/perfective sense (here functioning like ‘did/was doing’). تخت (throne) with کردن forms the compound verbal idea تخت کردن = “to enthrone” or “to set on the throne.” آرزوی at the end of this segment is not the object of تخت کردن; it is the noun that will serve as the grammatical subject of the next clause (its predicate follows in the next segment).
segments.s769-g42: دگرگونه‌تر شد is a predicative phrase: دگرگونه‌تر (more different) + شد (became) = “became more different.” That predicate takes آرزوی from the previous segment as its subject. به + آیین و خوی uses به in the sense “in/with respect to” and links two nouns (آیین = custom/usage, خوی = disposition/temper) joined by و, describing the domain in which the desire changed.
segments.s769-g43: تن خویش is the possessive phrase “his own body”; in the tokenization here خوی + ش represents خویش (own/his own), so the element ش is the possessive clitic. یک چند is an adverbial phrase meaning “for a short time / somewhat / a little,” and بیمار کرد is a causative past: بیمار + کردن = “to cause (someone) to become ill.” The subject is omitted and carried over from context (the agent who caused himself to become ill).
segments.s769-g44: پرستیدن is the infinitive/masdar “to serve/worship” and here functions as a noun phrase (the thing affected). شاه is the direct object of that verbal noun (no را is required). دشوار کرد is a causative construction: دشوار (difficult) + کرد (“made”) = “made (it) difficult.” Grammatically the whole phrase says that the preceding illness made serving the king difficult.
segments.s769-g9: پیاده شد (پیاده + شد) is a compound idea: پیاده here signals 'on foot / dismounted' and شد is the past of شدن used as the main past-forming verb, so together 'he dismounted / went on foot.' از introduces the source 'from' and bāreh (باره) here names the litter/palanquin (an older usage of بار/باره), followed by the proper name ماهوی (Māhavi). زود is an adverb modifying the whole verb phrase, 'quickly/soon.' Word order is typical older Persian: verb after its modifiers, and a noun phrase ('از باره ماهوی') follows the verb as its complement.
segments.s769-g10: بران is an archaic fused prepositional phrase meaning 'بر آن' = 'thereupon/after that'; کهتری is a substantive/adjective 'lower, more humble', qualifying بندگیها (plural of بندگی, 'acts of servitude/devotion'). فزود is a past-tense form meaning 'increased' or 'multiplied.' The segment as a whole states a consequent action: 'thereupon the lesser/humble acts of devotion increased.'
segments.s769-g11: همی‌رفت is the imperfective/progressive past ('he was going' / 'he moved'), here used as a simple past narrative marker; نرم is an adverbial attribute 'gently.' از بر + خاک گرم functions as a spatial phrase — from/on to the warm ground — with بر marking the surface ('onto/on') and خاک گرم 'warm earth' as the goal/location. The verb precedes its directional complement, showing the typical older Persian ordering.
segments.s769-g12: دو دیده = 'both eyes' (دو + دیده). پر از آب کرده is a small clause where پر از آب (full of water/tears) is completed by کرده (past participle/causative sense 'made / made to be'), so 'had made both eyes full of water' = 'his eyes filled with tears.' ز (poetic form of از) شرم gives the cause 'from shame' or 'out of modesty/embarrassment.' The whole phrase is a circumstantial clause describing his appearance as he moved.
segments.s769-g13: زمین را ببوسید is a straightforward past action 'he kissed the ground' (را marks definite direct object). و links to the next clause. بردش نماز is an idiomatic use of بردن with نماز: here برد(ن) + (ِ)ش (object clitic) + نماز means 'he performed/said the prayer' or 'he set about praying'; the lexical entry given for برد in the word list doesn't match this verbal use so the sense is the idiomatic verb 'to perform' with نماز as its object.
segments.s769-g14: همی‌بود is the imperfective/past habitual 'he was / he continued to be.' پیشش is a locative 'before him/her / in his/her presence' (here referring back to the addressee), followed by زمانی دراز 'for a long time' (زمانی = 'for a while/time; دراز = 'long'). The clause states duration/continuation: he remained there in that presence for a long time.
segments.s769-g15: فرخ‌زاد is the subject (a proper name). چون before a verb here functions temporally 'when/after' (classical/poetic use). روی ماهوی is the object phrase 'the face of Māhavi' (روی = face; ماهوی is a proper name here, not the abstract adjective). دید is the simple past 'saw.' Word order is Subject — temporal clause — object — verb, common in older Persian narrative.
segments.s769-g16: سپاهی is a collective noun 'soldiers / the soldiery.' بران ( = بر آن) + سان = 'in that way/like that' (بران سان = 'on that manner'). رده برکشید is a verbal phrase: رده (row/line/rank) + برکشید ('he drew up/raised'), so together 'he drew up a line of soldiers' or 'he formed his ranks.' The verb برکشید is past tense and completes the action triggered by seeing her.
segments.s769-g25: This clause opens with the subordinating particle که (here 'that/for/when'), followed by چون which functions temporally ('when/as') and the first-person subject من. فراوان is an adverb modifying the verb phrase that follows (meaning 'often' or 'in great number'). به + آوردگاه gives the goal/place 'to the battlefield' (به = 'to', آوردگاه = 'battlefield'). The whole chunk sets a temporal/location frame for the next clause.
segments.s769-g26: شد is the past (perfective) use of شدن ('became' / 'was'), here introducing the result clause. از جنگ = 'from/of the battle' (از = 'from'), آن modifies نیزه‌داران as 'those'/'that' spearmen, and تباه is a predicative adjective meaning 'ruined/destroyed'. The verb+adjective literal structure is 'became ruined'.
segments.s769-g27: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. رستم is the proper name; سواری is a noun meaning 'rider/horseman' and here characterizes Rostam. به گیتی = 'in the world' (به used idiomatically as 'in'), and نبود is the past negative 'was not'. The clause says that in the world there was no horseman like Rostam.
segments.s769-g28: This is a verbless/nominal clause typical of older Persian poetry: نه + [گوش خردمند] + هرگز + شنود. گوش here means 'ear' but idiomatically 'listener/attention'; خردمند qualifies it ('wise listener'). هرگز 'never' negates, and شنود (classical present form of شنیدن) is used narratively to mean 'heard' or 'ever learned of'—a general/presentive use common in epic style. Together: 'no wise ear ever heard.'
segments.s769-g29: بدست is the agent marker 'by' (literally 'by the hand of'), followed by یکی 'a certain/someone' and زاغ‌سر 'magpie/crow-headed bird' as the agent. کشته شد is the passive construction 'was killed' (کشته = 'killed', شد functioning as the auxiliary forming the past passive).
segments.s769-g30: به من بر چنین روز برگشته شد is an impersonal/experiential phrasing: به من = 'to me', بر ... روز = 'upon such a day' (بر = 'upon'), برگشته شد literally 'was returned' but idiomatically 'came back upon / befell'. The clause means 'such a day befell me' or 'I experienced such a day.' شد here completes the verbal phrase as the past event marker.
segments.s769-g31: که introduces the clause as a prayer/wish. یزدان 'God' is the subject; ورا = 'to him' (wrā = to/for him). جای نیکان = 'a place among the good/righteous' (جای = place, نیکان = the good). دهاد is an archaic/poetic form of 'دَهَد' (he gives / may give) used optatively here ('may he grant'). The whole is a blessing/wish: 'May God give him a place among the righteous.'
segments.s769-g32: This parallel clause names سیه‌زاغ (the black crow) as the direct object (را marks it), then gives the consequence: درد پیکان = 'the pain/wound of an arrow' (درد = pain/wound, پیکان = arrow). The verb دهاد repeats the optative/archaic 'may give'/'gave' sense: 'may He give the black crow the pain of the arrow' (i.e., may the crow suffer an arrow's wound).
segments.s769-g17: ز is a causal/ablative preposition here (‘because of’ / ‘from’), introducing the reason. ماهوی سوری is a compound epithet describing appearance (moon-like, ماهوی, qualified as rosy/scarlet, سوری). دلش = دل + ش is the possessed noun ‘his heart.’ گشت is the past inchoative ‘became’ (poetic alternative to شد) and شاد is the predicate adjective ‘glad/happy’ (pattern: [cause] + [possessed subject] + گشت + adjective).
segments.s769-g18: برو بر here functions as a discourse/temporal connector meaning ‘thereupon’ / ‘then’ (beyond its literal sense ‘go’); بر participates in that adverbial phrase. بسی is a quantifier (‘much/many’ in sense of ‘a great many’), پندها is the plural object ‘admonitions/advices,’ and کرد یاد is a light-verb construction meaning ‘made remembrance’ → ‘recalled’ (کرد is the past ‘made/did’ taking یاد ‘remembrance’ as its noun complement). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic meter but the whole means ‘thereupon he remembered many admonitions.’
segments.s769-g19: 'که' is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the clause; 'این شاه را' is a definite direct object phrase ('this king' + object marker را) placed before the verb in classical word order; 'از نژاد کیان' is a genitival/partitive phrase meaning 'of the lineage/house of the Kians' where 'از' carries the meaning 'of' and links 'نژاد' (lineage) to the proper name 'کیان'.
segments.s769-g20: 'سپردم' is the first-person past tense 'I entrusted/handed over'; the following 'تو را' is the person to whom something was entrusted (object/recipient marked by را); 'تا' here means 'so that / in order that' and introduces the purpose clause; 'ببندی' is a present/subjunctive-form verb 'you may bind/fasten' addressing the same 'تو'; 'میان' is a locative/adverbial 'between / in the middle' completing the purpose clause. The clause order (object + verb + recipient + purpose) reflects classical Persian inversion and poetic diction.
segments.s769-g21: 'نباید' is an impersonal modal meaning 'it must not / one should not'; 'که' introduces the clause stating what should not happen; 'بادی' (literally 'a wind / breeze') functions as the subject in this clause; 'برو' is the poetic/present form of 'to go' (here 'goes/should go') used impersonally; 'بر' is a preposition 'against/on' and takes 'جهد' ('effort / striving') as its object — together 'برو بر جهد' = 'go against effort'.
segments.s769-g22: 'وگر' = 'but if / and if' introduces a conditional; 'خود' here emphasizes the agent ('oneself' / 'itself'); 'سپاسی' is a noun 'praise / thanks' used as the thing that might be placed; 'برو' again functions as the verbal element in a poetic periphrasis and is followed by 'برنهد' (perfective/infinitive-like 'to place on/lay upon'), so the phrase expresses 'place praise upon oneself.' The whole clause means 'and if (one) were to place praise on oneself', with a negative or prohibitory sense carried from the first clause.
segments.s769-g23: The phrase 'مرا رفت باید' is a classical Persian impersonal construction meaning 'it is necessary for me to go' (literally: 'to me, going is necessary'); 'مرا' marks the experiencer/oblique 'me' with the object marker را; 'رفت' is the verbal stem 'go'; 'باید' is the modal 'must/ought'; 'همی' is an emphatic/adverbial particle 'indeed/now/still'; 'سوی ری' means 'toward Rey' ('سوی' = toward, 'ری' = the city Rey). The overall sense: 'I must (indeed) go toward Rey.'
segments.s769-g24: 'ندانم' is the negative first-person present 'I do not know'; 'که' introduces the subordinate interrogative clause; the first 'کی' here functions as 'when' ('when I shall see'); 'بینم' is first-person subjunctive/present 'I may see / I shall see' used for a future sense; the phrase 'این تاج کی' is an inverted verbless interrogative 'this crown — who?' i.e. 'who will wear/possess this crown?' Placing 'کی' after 'این تاج' is a common poetic inversion meaning 'who is the owner/wearer of this crown.'
segments.s769-g33: بدو گفت = گفت (past tense ‘said’) with the dative pronoun بدو ('to him') placed before the verb; ماهوی is an epithet (literally 'moon‑faced' or an epithet used as a name) identifying the speaker; کای is an archaic/poetic vocative marker ('O'); پهلوان is the noun addressed ('champion, hero'). The sequence shows the addressee and speaker before the quoted address: 'He, the moon‑faced one, said to him, "O champion..."'.
segments.s769-g34: مرا here functions as an experiencer/beneficiary pronoun ('for me' / 'to me'); شاه is the nominal subject 'the king'; چشمست is a contracted poetic copular phrase (چشم + است) meaning 'is an eye' or 'is sight' — i.e. 'is my eye' — and the copula is embedded in the single word چشمست; و links a second predicate; روشن‌روان is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'bright‑souled' or 'light‑spirited'. The predication is verbless in modern shape but uses an archaic fused copula and places the experiencer before the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s769-g35: پذیرفتم is first‑person singular past 'I accepted / I consented'. این زینهار is the direct object 'this warning / this admonition' (این = 'this', زینهار = 'warning'). تو را follows as a beneficiary/ethical dative with the object marker را, emphasizing 'for you' or 'as regards you'. The clause literally reads 'I accepted this warning for you.'
segments.s769-g36: This segment contains two nominal phrases with the repeated postposed تو را ('for you' / 'you (accusative/emphatic)') after each head noun: سپهر تو را (the heavens/sky — for you) and شهریار تو را (the sovereign/king — for you). The copula is omitted (a common poetic ellipsis), so each noun is understood predicatively or distributively as 'is yours' or 'belongs to you.'
segments.s769-g37: فرخ‌زاد هرمزد is a compound personal name (two names in apposition). زان جایگاه is a prepositional phrase: زان = از + آن ('from that'), جایگاه = 'place' (or position), so 'from that place.' The whole noun phrase identifies who moved and from where.
segments.s769-g38: سوی ری بیامد is the main verbal clause: سوی + place = 'toward / to Rey', and بیامد is the past of آمدن ('came'). به فرمان شاه is an instrumental/agentive phrase 'by the command/order of the king' (به here = 'by'/'by means of'). Word order is typical of epic Persian: direction + place + verb, then the phrase indicating cause/authority.
segments.s769-g39: برین نیز بگذشت چندی سپهر is a temporal clause: برین ('on this' / 'about this'), نیز 'also', بگذشت 'passed' (intransitive past: 'time passed'), چندی 'for a while'. In Persian epic style the time/verb cluster precedes or interrupts the subject; here سپهر ('the heavens/sky') follows, forming the temporal statement 'Some time passed concerning this, (as for) the heavens...'.
segments.s769-g40: جدا شد is a compound verbal predicate 'separated / parted' (جدا = apart, شد = became). ز is the preposition 'from'; مغز بداندیش is a genitive/possessive construction 'the mind/heart of the ill‑minded (one)'; مهر is here a proper name (the villain Mehr) or a person, so the whole means 'the heavens separated from the malicious Mehr's mind/heart.' The order is poetic and the copular/causal sense is carried by جدا شد.
segments.s769-g1: وزان is a poetic fused prepositional phrase (و + از آن) meaning “and from that,” placed before the noun for emphasis. جایگه is the noun ‘place’ (direct object/locative). برکشیدند is a past, 3rd-person-plural perfective verb ‘they raised’ (برکشیدن) and here takes کوس (war-drum) as its object; Persian allows the object after the verb in poetic order.
segments.s769-g2: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’. بست here is functioning as a place-name in the line (not one of the indexed senses), so its listed verbal senses do not apply. و simply links two place-names. شد is the past of شدن used intransitively (‘happened/was/arrived’), and تا … به طوس ('تا … به' + place) means ‘as far as / up to Tus’ (تا = ‘as far as’, به = ‘towards/into’).
segments.s769-g3: خبر یافت is a two-word verbal cluster: خبر (news) + یافت (past of یافتن ‘to obtain/receive’), so ‘received news’ or ‘learned.’ ماهوی سوری here is a proper name (Mahuy Suri) functioning as the experiencer/subject; the dictionary senses listed are semantic, so the proper-name reading is not covered by those senses. ز شاه is the source-agent ‘from the king.’
segments.s769-g4: که introduces the content clause (‘that’) reporting what was learned. تا مرز طوس = ‘as far as the border of Tus’ (تا = ‘as far as’; مرز = ‘border’; طوس = place). اندر آمد سپاه is inverted poetic order: آمد (past of آمدن ‘came’) followed by the subject سپاه (‘the army’), meaning ‘the army came/arrived within/up to…’. اندر = ‘in/within’.
segments.s769-g5: پذیره شدش is a compound where پذیره (reception/acceptance) plus شدش (شد + enclitic ش indicating the experiencer) yields a passive/experiential sense: ‘he was received’ or ‘reception befell him.’ با introduces the accompanying instrument/means ‘with’; سپاه گران is a noun + adjective ‘a heavy/mighty army’ (گران modifying سپاه).
segments.s769-g6: همه is an emphatic quantifier ‘all’ modifying the following nouns. نیزه‌داران (spear-bearers) and جوشن‌وران (armored/ cuirass‑wearing warriors) are a coordinated appositive list naming the composition of that host; adjectives/compound nouns follow the head in Persian poetic order.
segments.s769-g7: چو is the subordinating conjunction ‘when.’ پیدا شد is the past passive/intransitive expression ‘appeared / became manifest’ (پیدا = ‘visible/manifest’, شد = past of شدن). آن فر و اورند شاه is a noun phrase in apposition: آن فر (‘that farr/royal glory’) and اورند (here read as the proper name Orvand or an epithet) tied to شاه (‘the king’). The phrase groups the manifestation of divine/royal glory with the king.
segments.s769-g8: درفش بزرگی literally ‘a banner of greatness’ (درفش = standard/banner; بزرگی = greatness, here adjectival/qualifying), and چندان سپاه = ‘and so great an army’ (چندان = ‘to that extent/so much’). The two noun phrases are coordinated by و and describe the king’s visible retinue and emblem; word order places the qualifying noun after the head for poetic effect.

sentences.s769-s21: He enthroned the youths; the desire became more different in custom and disposition.
sentences.s769-s22: He made his body somewhat ill; this made serving the king difficult.
sentences.s769-s5: He quickly dismounted from Māhavi's litter; thereafter his humble acts of servitude increased.
sentences.s769-s6: He moved gently onto the warm earth, his two eyes filled with water from shame.
sentences.s769-s7: He kissed the ground and offered prayer; he remained before her for a long while.
sentences.s769-s8: When Farrokhzād saw Māhavi's face, he drew his soldiers up in that fashion.
sentences.s769-s13: For when I often went to the battlefield, those spearmen were ruined by that battle.
sentences.s769-s14: When Rostam there was no horseman in the world; no wise ear ever heard (of such).
sentences.s769-s15: He was slain by the hand of a certain magpie; such a day came upon me.
sentences.s769-s16: May God give him a place among the righteous, and may He give the black crow the pain of an arrow.
sentences.s769-s9: Because of her rosy, moonlike face his heart became glad; thereupon he recalled many admonitions.
sentences.s769-s10: That I have entrusted this king of the Kian line to you, so that you may bind him in the middle.
sentences.s769-s11: One should not let a breeze oppose effort, nor should one heap praise upon oneself.
sentences.s769-s12: I must go now toward Rey; I do not know when I shall see who will wear this crown.
sentences.s769-s17: The moon‑faced one said to him, "O champion, the king is my eye and bright‑souled."
sentences.s769-s18: I accept this warning; for you — the heavens; for you — the ruler (the king).
sentences.s769-s19: Farrokhzād Hormozd, from that place, came toward Rey by the king's command.
sentences.s769-s20: Some time also passed on this; the heavens parted from the wicked‑minded Mehr's heart.
sentences.s769-s1: And from that place they raised the war-drum; from Bust and Nishapur the host moved as far as Tus.
sentences.s769-s2: Mahuy Suri learned the news from the king that the army had come as far as the border of Tus.
sentences.s769-s3: He was received with a mighty host — all spear-bearers and armored warriors.
sentences.s769-s4: When that farr (royal glory) and Orvand appeared, the king displayed a great standard and so great a host.
